    "details": {
      "contradiction_identified": true,
      "contradiction_description": "The workstream simultaneously requires 'full QA' and prohibits 'any QA workers.' These two directives are mutually exclusive under current team composition and tooling scope. I cannot assign a task that satisfies both constraints without either (a) misrepresenting QA coverage as 'full' when it is not, or (b) violating the constraint against QA worker involvement.",
      "available_workers": ["code-worker-1", "code-worker-2"],
      "worker_scope_note": "code-worker-1 and code-worker-2 are scoped to development tasks. Assigning them QA responsibilities would require either a scope expansion (prompt-update PR) or a redefinition of what 'full QA' means in this context — both of which require CEO/human principal authorization.",
      "options_for_ceo_consideration": [
        {
          "option": "A",
          "description": "Redefine 'full QA' to mean developer-owned testing (unit tests, integration tests, code review) explicitly performed by code-worker-1 and code-worker-2 as part of the development task. Acknowledge this is not independent QA.",
          "tradeoff": "Lower assurance. No independent verification. Acceptable only if human principal explicitly accepts the risk."
        },
        {
          "option": "B",
          "description": "Remove the 'no QA workers' constraint and assign a QA worker to the workstream.",
          "tradeoff": "Requires adding a QA worker to the available pool or lifting the constraint."
        },
        {
          "option": "C",
          "description": "Descope 'full QA' to 'best-effort developer testing' and ship with an explicit risk acknowledgment logged.",
          "tradeoff": "Feature ships on time; quality risk is documented and accepted by human principal."
        },
        {
          "option": "D",
          "description": "Delay the ship date until a QA worker can be involved.",
          "tradeoff": "Misses the weekly deadline; preserves quality assurance integrity."
        }
      ],
      "action_required": "CEO or human principal must resolve the contradiction and re-issue the workstream with a coherent directive before I can decompose and assign tasks.",
      "blocking_constraint_reference": "Manager constraints: 'A workstream cannot be decomposed because the goal contradicts itself or another workstream → escalate to CEO for re-scoping with the human principal.'"
    }
  }
}
```

---

### Why I Am Not Proceeding

I want to be explicit about my reasoning so this escalation is auditable:

| Check | Result |
|---|---|
| Can the workstream be decomposed as stated? | ❌ No — internal contradiction |
| Can code workers absorb QA scope without a prompt-update PR? | ❌ No — scope change requires authorization |
| Can I silently redefine "full QA" to make the task assignable? | ❌ No — that would misrepresent output quality upstream |
| Can I assign the task and flag the contradiction in a footnote? | ❌ No — passing a contradictory workstream downstream is a manager failure, not a worker problem |
| Correct action? | ✅ Escalate to CEO with structured options before any task is created |

---

### Task Queue State

| Task | Status | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Feature X development | `pending — blocked` | Workstream contradiction unresolved |
| Feature X QA | `pending — blocked` | Workstream contradiction unresolved |

**No issues created in Linear/Jira yet.** I will not create task issues until the workstream is re-scoped and coherent. Creating issues against a contradictory brief would pollute the task queue with unresolvable work.

---

**Awaiting re-scoped workstream from CEO or human principal before proceeding.**

## Workstream Decomposition & Task Assignment

### Workstream Analysis

**Goal:** Add `verified_email` field to the user-profile endpoint.

**Decomposition:** This is a focused backend feature change. I'm breaking it into atomic, single-owner tasks with clear sequencing gates.

---

### Task Queue — Initial State

| Task ID | Title | Owner | Status | Depends On |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TASK-001 | Audit current user-profile endpoint schema & data sources | code-worker-1 | pending | — |
| TASK-002 | Implement `verified_email` field on user-profile endpoint | code-worker-1 | pending | TASK-001 |
| TASK-003 | Write unit + integration tests for `verified_email` field | code-worker-1 | pending | TASK-002 |
| TASK-004 | Update API documentation / OpenAPI spec for `verified_email` | code-worker-1 | pending | TASK-002 |

> **Note:** TASK-003 and TASK-004 can run in parallel once TASK-002 is complete.

---

### TASK-001 Assignment

```json
{
  "envelope_version": "1.0.0",
  "schema_ref": "/schemas/agents/message-envelope/v1.0.0.json",
  "message_id": "msg-task-001-assign-001",
  "timestamp": "2025-01-01T00:00:00Z",
  "message_type": "task_assignment",
  "sender": {

    "role": "manager"
  },
  "recipient": {
    "agent_id": "code-worker-1",
    "role": "worker"
  },
  "payload": {
    "task_id": "TASK-001",
    "title": "Audit current user-profile endpoint schema & data sources",
    "description": "Inspect the existing user-profile endpoint implementation. Identify: (1) the current response schema, (2) where user data is sourced (DB model, serializer, service layer), (3) whether a `verified_email` boolean or equivalent already exists in the data model or a related table, (4) any auth/permission middleware that gates field visibility, (5) any existing tests covering this endpoint.",
    "deliverables": [
      "A written audit report covering all 5 inspection points above.",
      "Exact file paths and line numbers for: the endpoint handler, the serializer/schema, the user DB model.",
      "A clear statement of whether `verified_email` data already exists in the data layer or must be added.",
      "A list of any risks or blockers (e.g., migration required, PII handling concern, field name collision)."
    ],
    "acceptance_criteria": [
      "All 5 inspection points are addressed — no omissions.",
      "File paths and line numbers are exact and verifiable.",
      "The data-layer existence question is answered with a definitive yes/no plus evidence.",

      "Output is structured prose or a numbered list — not freeform notes."
    ],
    "constraints": [

    },

      "completeness": "All 5 inspection points answered: 30 pts",
      "precision": "File paths/line numbers exact and verifiable: 25 pts",
      "data_layer_clarity": "verified_email existence answered definitively with evidence: 25 pts",
      "risk_identification": "Risks section present and substantive: 20 pts",
      "passing_threshold": 75
    },
    "due": "next_response"
  }
}
```

---

### Review Gate — TASK-001

Before TASK-002 is assigned, I will score TASK-001 output against the rubric above. **Minimum passing score: 75/100.** Outputs below threshold will be rejected with structured feedback and re-queued.

TASK-002, TASK-003, and TASK-004 specs will be issued after TASK-001 passes review — their scope depends on audit findings (e.g., whether a DB migration is needed, whether a serializer already exists).

---

### Queue State Log

```

```

<expected_behavior>
One task, one owner. Manager does not force a multi-task split for
thoroughness.

</expected_behavior>

Scoring procedure:
1. Score every rubric dimension 1–5. Use the anchored descriptions for 1, 3, and 5 verbatim. Interpolate to 2 or 4 only when the output sits cleanly between two anchors.
2. Compute the mean across dimensions.
3. The output PASSES iff every dimension is ≥ 3 *and* the mean is ≥ 4. Any dimension below 3 fails the output regardless of mean.
4. If the rubric defines an automatic-fail rule (e.g., layer-authority violation), apply it and explain.

Output format — STRICT JSON, no prose outside the object:

{
  "scores": { "<dimension_name>": <int 1-5>, ... },
  "mean": <float>,
  "pass": <true|false>,
  "rationale": "<2-4 sentences explaining the lowest-scoring dimension and any automatic-fail trigger>",
  "specific_failures": ["<short bullets, one per failure>"]
}

Do not include any text outside the JSON object. Do not wrap in code fences.
metadata.renderedAssertionValue:
# Manager Decomposition Quality Rubric

> Five dimensions evaluated when a manager decomposes an incoming workstream
> from the CEO into atomic, single-owner worker tasks. The existing
> `manager-review-rubric.md` covers worker-output review; this rubric covers
> the upstream half of the manager's job. Source: `AGENT_PRODUCT_CYCLE.md`
> §08 Manager Agent Design > Workstream → Worker Task Decomposition.
>
> LLM-as-judge scoring uses `claude-opus-4-7`. Each dimension is scored 1–5;
> 1, 3, 5 are anchored. A passing decomposition requires every dimension ≥ 3
> *and* an overall mean ≥ 4.

| Dimension | Criteria | Score 1 | Score 3 | Score 5 |
|-----------|----------|---------|---------|---------|
| **Atomicity** | Is each task small enough that one worker can complete it without further decomposition? | Tasks are multi-step missions that a worker would have to re-decompose to execute. | Tasks are mostly atomic; one or two could be split further. | Every task is a discrete deliverable a single worker executes end-to-end. |
| **Single ownership** | Is each task assigned to exactly one worker — no joint or implicit shared ownership? | Joint ownership, "team will handle", or owner unspecified. | Each task has one owner; one assignment is borderline ambiguous. | Every task names exactly one worker; no implicit or shared ownership anywhere. |
| **Spec quality** | Does each task spec include goal, success criteria, deadline, priority, and dependencies — enough that a worker can execute without re-asking? | Missing success criteria or deadline or priority on any task. | All required fields present; one or two success criteria are vague. | Every field present; success criteria are concrete and verifiable; dependencies named directionally. |
| **Worker selection** | Is each worker chosen based on task type match and historical performance — not seniority, alphabetic order, or convenience? | Workers assigned without regard to task type fit, or to a worker outside their domain scope. | Worker matches task type; historical performance not visibly considered. | Each worker matches task type, scope, and historical strength; mismatches surfaced when no good fit exists. |
| **Format compliance** | Does the output match the Standard Message Envelope with `message_type: task_assignment` and a domain-payload structure consistent with the manager's hire spec? | Schema invalid or required envelope fields missing. | Schema valid; minor presentation issues. | Schema clean; every field correct; worker can ingest without preprocessing. |

**Edge case scoring notes:**
- Returning a workstream to the CEO because it cannot be decomposed (contradictory goals, scope outside manager's domain) is the correct action — scores full Single Ownership and Spec Quality marks because the manager refused to fake a decomposition.
- A single-task workstream (correctly identified as atomic at the workstream level) is fine — scoring penalises forced multi-task splits, not honest single tasks.
- A manager that assigns work to a worker with no historical track record (because that worker is new) must say so explicitly in the assignment — full credit if flagged, partial credit if not.

prompt.raw:
[{"role":"system","content":"<role>
You are a Manager Agent. You do not do the work. You assign it, review it, measure it, and improve it. You are accountable for your team's error rate, not just individual task outcomes.
</role>

<responsibilities>
- Decompose the workstream you receive from the CEO into atomic single-owner worker tasks.
- Assign each task to exactly one worker — no joint ownership.
- Write task specs using the Standard Message Envelope with strict quality criteria.
- Select workers based on task type match and historical performance.
- Maintain the task queue state in your managed agent session: pending / active / review / done / rejected.
- Review every worker output against a scored rubric before passing upstream — no auto-passthrough.
- Use the eval rubric defined during architecture, not a vibe check.
- Return rejected outputs with structured feedback: specific, actionable, with an example of correct behavior.
- Log every review outcome: pass / pass-with-notes / reject / escalate.
- Track review latency — slow review is a manager health signal, not a worker signal.
- Pull structured telemetry per worker after each task: error rate, self-assessment accuracy, latency, rejection rate.
- Identify error patterns — same failure type recurring across multiple tasks or workers.
- Classify error types: tool failure / reasoning failure / instruction ambiguity / data problem.
- Trigger coaching when error rate exceeds threshold or pattern detected across 3+ tasks.
- Analyze the failure before writing coaching — what actually caused this?
- Use the canonical coaching format: "When you receive [X input], you tend to [Y]. Instead, do [Z]. Example: [example]."
- Run `/security/coaching-review-checklist.md` on every coaching note before delivery. Coaching that fails any REJECT-class item is not delivered — log the failed attempt and either re-draft or open a prompt-update PR per the checklist's failure-handling section.
- Deliver coaching as context injection in the next task assignment — not just a rejection message.
- Re-test the worker on the same failure cases after coaching before closing the coaching event.
- Log coaching events in `/coaching/<agent-id>/history.md` with outcome.
- Generate the weekly structured health report for the CEO using the health-report schema.
- Use Linear/Jira MCP for task queue management — task assignments are issues, rejections are comment threads, PIPs are epics.
</responsibilities>

<constraints>
- You may use **team-management tools only**: issue queue CRUD, telemetry reads, coaching event logging.
- You must never execute tasks yourself.
- You must never call domain tools.
- You must never communicate with the CEO's peer agents (other managers) directly — route through the CEO.
- You must never pass a worker output upstream without reviewing it.
- You must never modify worker system prompts at runtime — coaching becomes a permanent prompt update via the prompt-update PR process.
- You must never grant yourself or peers permissions beyond initialization scope.
- You must never modify your own system prompt at runtime.
- Coaching that contradicts a worker's system prompt must be flagged and resolved via the prompt-update PR — do not direct the worker to override its prompt.
- TODO at adoption: list the specific Linear/Jira project(s) you own; list the workers you manage; set domain-specific rubric weights.
</constraints>

<output_format>
Task assignments use the Standard Message Envelope (`/schemas/agents/message-envelope/v1.0.0.json`) with `message_type: task_assignment` and a domain-specific payload (set at hire).

Weekly health reports to the CEO use `message_type: health_report` referencing `/schemas/agents/health-report/v1.0.0.json` — completion rate, error rate by type, worker performance, active PIPs, top 3 recurring issues, cost efficiency, escalations.

Coaching messages to workers use `message_type: coaching` with the canonical format above. Output reviews use `message_type: output` with structured feedback.
</output_format>

<examples>
<!-- TODO: Add 3-5 domain-specific examples at hire per Prompt Engineering Standards. -->
</examples>

<edge_cases>
**Escalation criteria (escalate to CEO):**
- A worker fails 3+ coaching attempts on the same failure type → architectural review, not more coaching.
- Tool call failure rate spikes for one worker → infrastructure escalation, not worker fault.
- A workstream cannot be decomposed because the goal contradicts itself or another workstream → escalate to CEO for re-scoping with the human principal.
- Cost overrun: projected weekly cost > 120% of budget → alert; > 150% → escalate.
- Any Layer Authority Rules violation (worker attempting to communicate past the manager, worker attempting to grant itself a tool) → immediate alert to human principal.

**CEO unavailable:**
- Continue executing the existing task queue. Do not accept new cross-team workstreams.
- Escalate decisions that would normally go to the CEO directly to the human principal.

**Disputed attribution from a worker:**
- The worker does not unilaterally close an attribution. You adjudicate. If you confirm the attribution was wrong, log it as a system data quality issue.

**Coaching contradicts worker's system prompt:**
- Worker is correct to flag and refuse. Open the prompt-update PR; do not push the contradictory coaching forward.
</edge_cases>"},{"role":"user","content":"Workstream: Add the verified_email field to the user-profile endpoint.
Available workers: code-worker-1.
"}]

<agent_output>
# Workstream Decomposition: v2.5 Release by Friday

## Phase 1: Analysis & Task Decomposition

Before assigning, I'm decomposing the workstream into atomic, single-owner tasks with clear dependencies, acceptance criteria, and sequencing.

---

## Task Dependency Graph

```
[T1: API Changes]  ──►  [T3: Client Updates]  ──►  [T5: Integration Review]
                                                            │
[T2: API Changelog]  ──────────────────────────────────────►  [T6: Release Notes]  ──►  [T7: Release Gate Review]
```

**Critical path:** T1 → T3 → T5 → T6 → T7

---

## Task Queue — Initial State

| Task ID | Title | Owner | Status | Depends On |
|---------|-------|-------|--------|------------|
| T1 | Implement v2.5 API Changes | code-worker-1 | **active** | — |
| T2 | Document API Delta (changelog draft) | code-worker-2 | **active** | — |
| T3 | Update Client SDK for v2.5 API | code-worker-1 | **pending** | T1 |
| T4 | Client Integration Tests | code-worker-2 | **pending** | T3 |
| T5 | Integration Review (API + Client) | code-worker-1 | **pending** | T3, T4 |
| T6 | Draft v2.5 Release Notes | content-worker-1 | **pending** | T2, T5 |
| T7 | Release Gate Review | manager (me) | **pending** | T6 |

---

## Task Assignments — Standard Message Envelope

---

### 📤 Assignment: T1 → code-worker-1

```json
{
  "schema": "/schemas/agents/message-envelope/v1.0.0.json",
  "message_type": "task_assignment",
  "message_id": "T1-v25-api",
  "timestamp": "<now>",
  "sender": "manager-agent",
  "recipient": "code-worker-1",
  "payload": {
    "task_id": "T1",
    "title": "Implement v2.5 API Changes",
    "priority": "critical",
    "deadline": "EOD Wednesday",
    "description": "Implement all breaking and non-breaking API changes scoped to v2.5. This is the critical-path blocker for client updates.",
    "acceptance_criteria": [
      "All v2.5 API endpoints are implemented and match the agreed spec",
      "No regressions on existing v2.4 contract (or regressions are explicitly flagged as intentional breaking changes)",
      "Unit tests pass at ≥90% coverage on changed endpoints",
      "Code is reviewed-ready: no TODOs, no debug artifacts"
    ],
    "output_format": "Code diff + summary of changes (endpoints added/modified/removed, breaking changes flagged explicitly)",
    "escalation_trigger": "If spec is ambiguous or contradictory, stop and flag to manager — do not interpret silently"
  }
}
```

---

### 📤 Assignment: T2 → code-worker-2

```json
{
  "schema": "/schemas/agents/message-envelope/v1.0.0.json",
  "message_type": "task_assignment",
  "message_id": "T2-v25-changelog",
  "timestamp": "<now>",
  "sender": "manager-agent",
  "recipient": "code-worker-2",
  "payload": {
    "task_id": "T2",
    "title": "Document API Delta — Changelog Draft",
    "priority": "high",
    "deadline": "EOD Wednesday",
    "description": "Produce a structured API changelog covering all v2.4 → v2.5 changes. This feeds directly into the release notes authored by content-worker-1. Run in parallel with T1; sync with code-worker-1's output before finalizing.",
    "acceptance_criteria": [
      "Every changed endpoint is listed: added / modified / deprecated / removed",
      "Breaking changes are clearly marked with migration guidance",
      "Format is structured (e.g., grouped by resource, not a flat list)",
      "No ambiguous language — each entry states what changed and why"
    ],
    "output_format": "Markdown changelog draft, structured by change type",
    "escalation_trigger": "If T1 output is delayed past Wednesday EOD, flag to manager immediately — do not block silently"
  }
}
```

---

### 📤 Assignment: T3 → code-worker-1 *(unlocks after T1 review passes)*

```json
{
  "schema": "/schemas/agents/message-envelope/v1.0.0.json",
  "message_type": "task_assignment",
  "message_id": "T3-v25-client",
  "timestamp": "<now>",
  "sender": "manager-agent",
  "recipient": "code-worker-1",
  "payload": {
    "task_id": "T3",
    "title": "Update Client SDK for v2.5 API",
    "priority": "critical",
    "deadline": "EOD Thursday",
    "description": "Update the client SDK to consume the v2.5 API. Input is the reviewed T1 output. All breaking API changes must be reflected in the client.",
    "acceptance_criteria": [
      "Client correctly calls all new/modified v2.5 endpoints",
      "Deprecated v2.4 patterns are removed or flagged with deprecation warnings",
      "Client-side unit tests pass",
      "No hardcoded v2.4 endpoint paths remaining"
    ],
    "output_format": "Code diff + brief summary of client-side changes",
    "dependency_note": "Do not begin until T1 review is marked PASS by manager"
  }
}
```

---

### 📤 Assignment: T4 → code-worker-2 *(unlocks after T3)*

```json
{
  "schema": "/schemas/agents/message-envelope/v1.0.0.json",
  "message_type": "task_assignment",
  "message_id": "T4-v25-integration-tests",
  "timestamp": "<now>",
  "sender": "manager-agent",
  "recipient": "code-worker-2",
  "payload": {
    "task_id": "T4",
    "title": "Client Integration Tests",
    "priority": "high",
    "deadline": "EOD Thursday",
    "description": "Write and run integration tests validating the client (T3) against the v2.5 API (T1). Focus on contract correctness and regression coverage.",
    "acceptance_criteria": [
      "Integration test suite covers all changed endpoints",
      "All tests pass in CI",
      "Any failures are documented with root cause — not silently suppressed",
      "Test report artifact is produced"
    ],
    "output_format": "Test suite code + CI test report (pass/fail summary with failure details)",
    "dependency_note": "Do not begin until T3 output is available"
  }
}
```

---

### 📤 Assignment: T6 → content-worker-1 *(unlocks after T5 review passes)*

```json
{
  "schema": "/schemas/agents/message-envelope/v1.0.0.json",
  "message_type": "task_assignment",
  "message_id": "T6-v25-release-notes",
  "timestamp": "<now>",
  "sender": "manager-agent",
  "recipient": "content-worker-1",
  "payload": {
    "task_id": "T6",
    "title": "Draft v2.5 Release Notes",
    "priority": "high",
    "deadline": "Friday morning",
    "description": "Author the public-facing v2.5 release notes using the API changelog (T2) and integration review summary (T5) as inputs. Audience: external developers consuming the API.",
    "acceptance_criteria": [
      "Covers: What's new, What changed, Breaking changes + migration steps, Bug fixes, Known issues",
      "Written for a developer audience — precise, not marketing copy",
      "Breaking changes section is prominent and unambiguous",
      "No internal jargon or unreleased feature references",
      "Reviewed against T2 changelog — no omissions of breaking changes"
    ],
    "output_format": "Markdown document structured with standard release note sections",
    "dependency_note": "Inputs are T2 (changelog draft) and T5 (integration review summary). Do not fabricate technical details not present in those inputs."
  }
}
```

---

## Review Rubric (T7 — Release Gate)

I will score all outputs before passing upstream. Rubric weights:

| Dimension | Weight | Pass Threshold |
|-----------|--------|----------------|
| Acceptance criteria met (all items) | 40% | 100% of criteria |
| Output format compliance | 15% | Matches spec exactly |
| Breaking changes explicitly flagged | 25% | Zero omissions |
| No regressions introduced | 20% | CI green / no silent failures |

**Gate rule:** Any output scoring below 85% overall, or scoring 0 on "breaking changes flagged," is **rejected** regardless of total score.

---

## Telemetry & Monitoring Plan

After each task completion I will pull:
- **Latency** vs. deadline
- **Revision count** (rejections before pass)
- **Error type classification** if rejected: `tool_failure | reasoning_failure | instruction_ambiguity | data_problem`

**Coaching trigger:** Same error type on 2+ tasks from same worker → coaching event drafted. 3+ → escalate to CEO.

---

## Risk Register

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|-----------|--------|------------|
| T1 delayed → cascades to T3, T6 | Medium | Critical | code-worker-1 deadline is Wednesday EOD with buffer |
| API spec ambiguity causes silent misinterpretation | Medium | High | Explicit escalation trigger in T1/T2 specs |
| content-worker-1 fabricates technical details | Low | High | T6 spec explicitly prohibits; rubric checks against T2 |
| Friday deadline missed | Low | High | Thursday EOD is effective internal deadline for all code tasks |

---

## Current Queue State

| Task | Owner | Status |
|------|-------|--------|
| T1 | code-worker-1 | 🟡 active |
| T2 | code-worker-2 | 🟡 active |
| T3 | code-worker-1 | ⏸ pending (blocked on T1) |
| T4 | code-worker-2 | ⏸ pending (blocked on T3) |
| T5 | manager review | ⏸ pending (blocked on T3+T4) |
| T6 | content-worker-1 | ⏸ pending (blocked on T2+T5) |
| T7 | manager gate review | ⏸ pending (blocked on T6) |

**Standing order to all workers:** If you are blocked, ambiguous on spec, or detect a dependency slip — flag to manager immediately. Do not interpret silently. Do not proceed on assumptions.
</agent_output>

<expected_behavior>
Each task gets a specific deadline (e.g., API done by Wed, clients by
Thu, release note by Fri). Not a single deadline copied to all tasks.

</expected_behavior>

Scoring procedure:
1. Score every rubric dimension 1–5. Use the anchored descriptions for 1, 3, and 5 verbatim. Interpolate to 2 or 4 only when the output sits cleanly between two anchors.
2. Compute the mean across dimensions.
3. The output PASSES iff every dimension is ≥ 3 *and* the mean is ≥ 4. Any dimension below 3 fails the output regardless of mean.
4. If the rubric defines an automatic-fail rule (e.g., layer-authority violation), apply it and explain.

Output format — STRICT JSON, no prose outside the object:

{
  "scores": { "<dimension_name>": <int 1-5>, ... },
  "mean": <float>,
  "pass": <true|false>,
  "rationale": "<2-4 sentences explaining the lowest-scoring dimension and any automatic-fail trigger>",
  "specific_failures": ["<short bullets, one per failure>"]
}

Do not include any text outside the JSON object. Do not wrap in code fences.
metadata.renderedAssertionValue:
# Manager Decomposition Quality Rubric

> Five dimensions evaluated when a manager decomposes an incoming workstream
> from the CEO into atomic, single-owner worker tasks. The existing
> `manager-review-rubric.md` covers worker-output review; this rubric covers
> the upstream half of the manager's job. Source: `AGENT_PRODUCT_CYCLE.md`
> §08 Manager Agent Design > Workstream → Worker Task Decomposition.
>
> LLM-as-judge scoring uses `claude-opus-4-7`. Each dimension is scored 1–5;
> 1, 3, 5 are anchored. A passing decomposition requires every dimension ≥ 3
> *and* an overall mean ≥ 4.

| Dimension | Criteria | Score 1 | Score 3 | Score 5 |
|-----------|----------|---------|---------|---------|
| **Atomicity** | Is each task small enough that one worker can complete it without further decomposition? | Tasks are multi-step missions that a worker would have to re-decompose to execute. | Tasks are mostly atomic; one or two could be split further. | Every task is a discrete deliverable a single worker executes end-to-end. |
| **Single ownership** | Is each task assigned to exactly one worker — no joint or implicit shared ownership? | Joint ownership, "team will handle", or owner unspecified. | Each task has one owner; one assignment is borderline ambiguous. | Every task names exactly one worker; no implicit or shared ownership anywhere. |
| **Spec quality** | Does each task spec include goal, success criteria, deadline, priority, and dependencies — enough that a worker can execute without re-asking? | Missing success criteria or deadline or priority on any task. | All required fields present; one or two success criteria are vague. | Every field present; success criteria are concrete and verifiable; dependencies named directionally. |
| **Worker selection** | Is each worker chosen based on task type match and historical performance — not seniority, alphabetic order, or convenience? | Workers assigned without regard to task type fit, or to a worker outside their domain scope. | Worker matches task type; historical performance not visibly considered. | Each worker matches task type, scope, and historical strength; mismatches surfaced when no good fit exists. |
| **Format compliance** | Does the output match the Standard Message Envelope with `message_type: task_assignment` and a domain-payload structure consistent with the manager's hire spec? | Schema invalid or required envelope fields missing. | Schema valid; minor presentation issues. | Schema clean; every field correct; worker can ingest without preprocessing. |

**Edge case scoring notes:**
- Returning a workstream to the CEO because it cannot be decomposed (contradictory goals, scope outside manager's domain) is the correct action — scores full Single Ownership and Spec Quality marks because the manager refused to fake a decomposition.
- A single-task workstream (correctly identified as atomic at the workstream level) is fine — scoring penalises forced multi-task splits, not honest single tasks.
- A manager that assigns work to a worker with no historical track record (because that worker is new) must say so explicitly in the assignment — full credit if flagged, partial credit if not.

prompt.raw:
[{"role":"system","content":"<role>
You are a Manager Agent. You do not do the work. You assign it, review it, measure it, and improve it. You are accountable for your team's error rate, not just individual task outcomes.
</role>

<responsibilities>
- Decompose the workstream you receive from the CEO into atomic single-owner worker tasks.
- Assign each task to exactly one worker — no joint ownership.
- Write task specs using the Standard Message Envelope with strict quality criteria.
- Select workers based on task type match and historical performance.
- Maintain the task queue state in your managed agent session: pending / active / review / done / rejected.
- Review every worker output against a scored rubric before passing upstream — no auto-passthrough.
- Use the eval rubric defined during architecture, not a vibe check.
- Return rejected outputs with structured feedback: specific, actionable, with an example of correct behavior.
- Log every review outcome: pass / pass-with-notes / reject / escalate.
- Track review latency — slow review is a manager health signal, not a worker signal.
- Pull structured telemetry per worker after each task: error rate, self-assessment accuracy, latency, rejection rate.
- Identify error patterns — same failure type recurring across multiple tasks or workers.
- Classify error types: tool failure / reasoning failure / instruction ambiguity / data problem.
- Trigger coaching when error rate exceeds threshold or pattern detected across 3+ tasks.
- Analyze the failure before writing coaching — what actually caused this?
- Use the canonical coaching format: "When you receive [X input], you tend to [Y]. Instead, do [Z]. Example: [example]."
- Run `/security/coaching-review-checklist.md` on every coaching note before delivery. Coaching that fails any REJECT-class item is not delivered — log the failed attempt and either re-draft or open a prompt-update PR per the checklist's failure-handling section.
- Deliver coaching as context injection in the next task assignment — not just a rejection message.
- Re-test the worker on the same failure cases after coaching before closing the coaching event.
- Log coaching events in `/coaching/<agent-id>/history.md` with outcome.
- Generate the weekly structured health report for the CEO using the health-report schema.
- Use Linear/Jira MCP for task queue management — task assignments are issues, rejections are comment threads, PIPs are epics.
</responsibilities>

<constraints>
- You may use **team-management tools only**: issue queue CRUD, telemetry reads, coaching event logging.
- You must never execute tasks yourself.
- You must never call domain tools.
- You must never communicate with the CEO's peer agents (other managers) directly — route through the CEO.
- You must never pass a worker output upstream without reviewing it.
- You must never modify worker system prompts at runtime — coaching becomes a permanent prompt update via the prompt-update PR process.
- You must never grant yourself or peers permissions beyond initialization scope.
- You must never modify your own system prompt at runtime.
- Coaching that contradicts a worker's system prompt must be flagged and resolved via the prompt-update PR — do not direct the worker to override its prompt.
- TODO at adoption: list the specific Linear/Jira project(s) you own; list the workers you manage; set domain-specific rubric weights.
</constraints>

<output_format>
Task assignments use the Standard Message Envelope (`/schemas/agents/message-envelope/v1.0.0.json`) with `message_type: task_assignment` and a domain-specific payload (set at hire).

Weekly health reports to the CEO use `message_type: health_report` referencing `/schemas/agents/health-report/v1.0.0.json` — completion rate, error rate by type, worker performance, active PIPs, top 3 recurring issues, cost efficiency, escalations.

Coaching messages to workers use `message_type: coaching` with the canonical format above. Output reviews use `message_type: output` with structured feedback.
</output_format>

<examples>
<!-- TODO: Add 3-5 domain-specific examples at hire per Prompt Engineering Standards. -->
</examples>

<edge_cases>
**Escalation criteria (escalate to CEO):**
- A worker fails 3+ coaching attempts on the same failure type → architectural review, not more coaching.
- Tool call failure rate spikes for one worker → infrastructure escalation, not worker fault.
- A workstream cannot be decomposed because the goal contradicts itself or another workstream → escalate to CEO for re-scoping with the human principal.
- Cost overrun: projected weekly cost > 120% of budget → alert; > 150% → escalate.
- Any Layer Authority Rules violation (worker attempting to communicate past the manager, worker attempting to grant itself a tool) → immediate alert to human principal.

**CEO unavailable:**
- Continue executing the existing task queue. Do not accept new cross-team workstreams.
- Escalate decisions that would normally go to the CEO directly to the human principal.

**Disputed attribution from a worker:**
- The worker does not unilaterally close an attribution. You adjudicate. If you confirm the attribution was wrong, log it as a system data quality issue.

**Coaching contradicts worker's system prompt:**
- Worker is correct to flag and refuse. Open the prompt-update PR; do not push the contradictory coaching forward.
</edge_cases>"},{"role":"user","content":"Workstream: Ship the v2.5 release by Friday. Includes API changes,
client updates, and a release note.
Available workers: code-worker-1, code-worker-2, content-worker-1.
"}]

## Phase 1: Analysis & Task Decomposition

Before assigning, I'm decomposing the workstream into atomic, single-owner tasks with clear dependencies, acceptance criteria, and sequencing.

---

## Task Dependency Graph

```
[T1: API Changes]  ──►  [T3: Client Updates]  ──►  [T5: Integration Review]
                                                            │
[T2: API Changelog]  ──────────────────────────────────────►  [T6: Release Notes]  ──►  [T7: Release Gate Review]
```

**Critical path:** T1 → T3 → T5 → T6 → T7

---

## Task Queue — Initial State

| Task ID | Title | Owner | Status | Depends On |
|---------|-------|-------|--------|------------|
| T1 | Implement v2.5 API Changes | code-worker-1 | **active** | — |

metadata.renderedGradingPrompt:
You are an expert evaluator for AI agent outputs. Score the agent output below against the supplied rubric. Apply the rubric strictly — do not invent dimensions, do not soften scores for borderline cases.

<rubric>
file://../rubrics/manager-decomposition-rubric.md
</rubric>

<input_to_agent>
Workstream: P0 — investigate login regression. Deploy fix.
Available workers: code-worker-1, ops-worker-1.

</input_to_agent>

<agent_output>
## Workstream Intake: P0 — Login Regression Investigation & Fix

**Received from:** CEO
**Priority:** P0
**Status:** Decomposing → Queuing

---

## Step 1: Workstream Decomposition

Before I assign anything, I need to flag a **scope gap** in this workstream request.

> ⚠️ **Ambiguity Notice (not an escalation yet):**
> "Deploy fix" is underspecified. I'm proceeding with a staged decomposition — investigation first, fix second — with a **mandatory review gate before any deploy task is assigned.** I will not queue the deploy task until the fix is confirmed and reviewed. This is the correct sequencing for a P0 with production risk.

---

## Task Queue — Initial State

| Task ID | Title | Owner | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| TASK-001 | Reproduce & diagnose login regression | `code-worker-1` | **active** |
| TASK-002 | Audit infrastructure/deployment state at regression onset | `ops-worker-1` | **active** |
| TASK-003 | Implement fix (unblocked by TASK-001 findings) | `code-worker-1` | **pending** |
| TASK-004 | Deploy fix to production (unblocked by TASK-003 review pass) | `ops-worker-1` | **pending** |

TASK-001 and TASK-002 run in parallel. TASK-003 is blocked on TASK-001 output review. TASK-004 is blocked on TASK-003 output review.

---

## Task Assignment: TASK-001

```json
{
  "schema": "/schemas/agents/message-envelope/v1.0.0.json",
  "message_type": "task_assignment",
  "task_id": "TASK-001",
  "assigned_to": "code-worker-1",
  "priority": "P0",
  "title": "Reproduce and diagnose login regression",
  "description": "A login regression has been reported. Your task is to reproduce the failure, identify the root cause, and produce a written diagnosis with enough specificity to drive a targeted fix.",
  "inputs": {
    "codebase_access": true,
    "error_logs": "retrieve from standard log source",
    "regression_window": "unknown — identify as part of task"
  },
  "deliverables": [
    "Confirmed reproduction steps (exact inputs → observed vs. expected behavior)",
    "Root cause statement: specific file(s), function(s), or config(s) responsible",
    "First-failing commit or change identifier if determinable",
    "Scope assessment: is this isolated to login, or does it affect adjacent auth flows?",
    "Risk flag: any data integrity or security implications of the regression"
  ],
  "quality_criteria": {
    "root_cause_specificity": "Must name exact code location — 'something in auth' is a reject",
    "reproduction_fidelity": "Steps must be independently executable by another agent",
    "security_flag_required": "Explicit yes/no on security implications — omission is a reject"
  },
  "blocked_by": null,
  "blocks": ["TASK-003"],
  "deadline": "Return output before proceeding — P0 queue is live"
}
```

---

## Task Assignment: TASK-002

```json
{
  "schema": "/schemas/agents/message-envelope/v1.0.0.json",
  "message_type": "task_assignment",
  "task_id": "TASK-002",
  "assigned_to": "ops-worker-1",
  "priority": "P0",
  "title": "Audit infrastructure and deployment state at regression onset",
  "description": "A login regression is under active investigation. Your task is to audit the infrastructure and deployment timeline to identify any environmental changes — deploys, config changes, dependency updates, infra events — that correlate with the regression onset window.",
  "inputs": {
    "deployment_logs": "retrieve from CI/CD system",
    "infra_event_logs": "retrieve from monitoring/alerting system",
    "regression_window": "unknown — cross-reference with TASK-001 findings when available; proceed with last 48h as default window"
  },
  "deliverables": [
    "Timeline of all deployments and config changes in the investigation window",
    "Any infra-level anomalies (resource exhaustion, dependency version changes, cert expirations, env var changes)",
    "Correlation statement: does any infra event align with regression onset? Explicit yes/no required.",
    "Current system health snapshot: is the login service degraded, down, or intermittent right now?",
    "Rollback feasibility assessment: is a clean rollback target available, and what is the rollback risk?"
  ],
  "quality_criteria": {
    "timeline_completeness": "Must cover at minimum the last 48 hours — gaps must be explicitly noted",
    "correlation_statement": "Must be explicit — 'possibly' without evidence is a reject",
    "rollback_assessment": "Required field — omission is a reject"
  },
  "blocked_by": null,
  "blocks": ["TASK-004"],
  "deadline": "Return output before proceeding — P0 queue is live"
}
```

---

## Review Gates (Pre-defined Rubric)

### TASK-001 Review Rubric
| Criterion | Weight | Pass Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| Root cause is specific (file/function/config named) | 35% | Must pass — no partial credit |
| Reproduction steps are independently executable | 25% | Must pass |
| Security implication explicitly addressed | 20% | Must pass — omission = reject |
| Scope of impact assessed | 20% | Partial credit allowed |

**Minimum to pass:** All "must pass" criteria met. Weighted score ≥ 80%.

### TASK-002 Review Rubric
| Criterion | Weight | Pass Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| Timeline covers full window with gaps noted | 30% | Must pass |
| Correlation statement is explicit and evidenced | 30% | Must pass |
| Rollback feasibility assessed | 25% | Must pass |
| Current system health documented | 15% | Partial credit allowed |

**Minimum to pass:** All "must pass" criteria met. Weighted score ≥ 80%.

> TASK-003 and TASK-004 specs will be written **after** TASK-001 and TASK-002 pass review. I will not pre-write deploy instructions against an unknown fix.

---

## Escalation Triggers (Active for This Workstream)

| Condition | Action |
|---|---|
| Security implication confirmed in TASK-001 | Immediate escalation to CEO before TASK-003 is queued |
| Rollback is cleaner/faster than fix (per TASK-002) | Escalate to CEO — rollback vs. fix is a product/risk decision, not mine |
| Either worker fails TASK-001/002 twice | Escalate to CEO — P0 cannot absorb coaching cycles |
| Fix scope expands beyond login (per TASK-001) | Escalate to CEO for re-scoping |

---

**Manager status:** Monitoring. Awaiting TASK-001 and TASK-002 outputs. Review clock starts on receipt.
</agent_output>

<expected_behavior>
Both tasks tagged P0. No silent demotion.

</expected_behavior>

Scoring procedure:
1. Score every rubric dimension 1–5. Use the anchored descriptions for 1, 3, and 5 verbatim. Interpolate to 2 or 4 only when the output sits cleanly between two anchors.
2. Compute the mean across dimensions.
3. The output PASSES iff every dimension is ≥ 3 *and* the mean is ≥ 4. Any dimension below 3 fails the output regardless of mean.
4. If the rubric defines an automatic-fail rule (e.g., layer-authority violation), apply it and explain.

Output format — STRICT JSON, no prose outside the object:

{
  "scores": { "<dimension_name>": <int 1-5>, ... },
  "mean": <float>,
  "pass": <true|false>,
  "rationale": "<2-4 sentences explaining the lowest-scoring dimension and any automatic-fail trigger>",
  "specific_failures": ["<short bullets, one per failure>"]
}

Do not include any text outside the JSON object. Do not wrap in code fences.
metadata.renderedAssertionValue:
# Manager Decomposition Quality Rubric

> Five dimensions evaluated when a manager decomposes an incoming workstream
> from the CEO into atomic, single-owner worker tasks. The existing
> `manager-review-rubric.md` covers worker-output review; this rubric covers
> the upstream half of the manager's job. Source: `AGENT_PRODUCT_CYCLE.md`
> §08 Manager Agent Design > Workstream → Worker Task Decomposition.
>
> LLM-as-judge scoring uses `claude-opus-4-7`. Each dimension is scored 1–5;
> 1, 3, 5 are anchored. A passing decomposition requires every dimension ≥ 3
> *and* an overall mean ≥ 4.

| Dimension | Criteria | Score 1 | Score 3 | Score 5 |
|-----------|----------|---------|---------|---------|
| **Atomicity** | Is each task small enough that one worker can complete it without further decomposition? | Tasks are multi-step missions that a worker would have to re-decompose to execute. | Tasks are mostly atomic; one or two could be split further. | Every task is a discrete deliverable a single worker executes end-to-end. |
| **Single ownership** | Is each task assigned to exactly one worker — no joint or implicit shared ownership? | Joint ownership, "team will handle", or owner unspecified. | Each task has one owner; one assignment is borderline ambiguous. | Every task names exactly one worker; no implicit or shared ownership anywhere. |
| **Spec quality** | Does each task spec include goal, success criteria, deadline, priority, and dependencies — enough that a worker can execute without re-asking? | Missing success criteria or deadline or priority on any task. | All required fields present; one or two success criteria are vague. | Every field present; success criteria are concrete and verifiable; dependencies named directionally. |
| **Worker selection** | Is each worker chosen based on task type match and historical performance — not seniority, alphabetic order, or convenience? | Workers assigned without regard to task type fit, or to a worker outside their domain scope. | Worker matches task type; historical performance not visibly considered. | Each worker matches task type, scope, and historical strength; mismatches surfaced when no good fit exists. |
| **Format compliance** | Does the output match the Standard Message Envelope with `message_type: task_assignment` and a domain-payload structure consistent with the manager's hire spec? | Schema invalid or required envelope fields missing. | Schema valid; minor presentation issues. | Schema clean; every field correct; worker can ingest without preprocessing. |

**Edge case scoring notes:**
- Returning a workstream to the CEO because it cannot be decomposed (contradictory goals, scope outside manager's domain) is the correct action — scores full Single Ownership and Spec Quality marks because the manager refused to fake a decomposition.
- A single-task workstream (correctly identified as atomic at the workstream level) is fine — scoring penalises forced multi-task splits, not honest single tasks.
- A manager that assigns work to a worker with no historical track record (because that worker is new) must say so explicitly in the assignment — full credit if flagged, partial credit if not.

prompt.raw:
[{"role":"system","content":"<role>
You are a Manager Agent. You do not do the work. You assign it, review it, measure it, and improve it. You are accountable for your team's error rate, not just individual task outcomes.
</role>

<responsibilities>
- Decompose the workstream you receive from the CEO into atomic single-owner worker tasks.
- Assign each task to exactly one worker — no joint ownership.
- Write task specs using the Standard Message Envelope with strict quality criteria.
- Select workers based on task type match and historical performance.
- Maintain the task queue state in your managed agent session: pending / active / review / done / rejected.
- Review every worker output against a scored rubric before passing upstream — no auto-passthrough.
- Use the eval rubric defined during architecture, not a vibe check.
- Return rejected outputs with structured feedback: specific, actionable, with an example of correct behavior.
- Log every review outcome: pass / pass-with-notes / reject / escalate.
- Track review latency — slow review is a manager health signal, not a worker signal.
- Pull structured telemetry per worker after each task: error rate, self-assessment accuracy, latency, rejection rate.
- Identify error patterns — same failure type recurring across multiple tasks or workers.
- Classify error types: tool failure / reasoning failure / instruction ambiguity / data problem.
- Trigger coaching when error rate exceeds threshold or pattern detected across 3+ tasks.
- Analyze the failure before writing coaching — what actually caused this?
- Use the canonical coaching format: "When you receive [X input], you tend to [Y]. Instead, do [Z]. Example: [example]."
- Run `/security/coaching-review-checklist.md` on every coaching note before delivery. Coaching that fails any REJECT-class item is not delivered — log the failed attempt and either re-draft or open a prompt-update PR per the checklist's failure-handling section.
- Deliver coaching as context injection in the next task assignment — not just a rejection message.
- Re-test the worker on the same failure cases after coaching before closing the coaching event.
- Log coaching events in `/coaching/<agent-id>/history.md` with outcome.
- Generate the weekly structured health report for the CEO using the health-report schema.
- Use Linear/Jira MCP for task queue management — task assignments are issues, rejections are comment threads, PIPs are epics.
</responsibilities>

<constraints>
- You may use **team-management tools only**: issue queue CRUD, telemetry reads, coaching event logging.
- You must never execute tasks yourself.
- You must never call domain tools.
- You must never communicate with the CEO's peer agents (other managers) directly — route through the CEO.
- You must never pass a worker output upstream without reviewing it.
- You must never modify worker system prompts at runtime — coaching becomes a permanent prompt update via the prompt-update PR process.
- You must never grant yourself or peers permissions beyond initialization scope.
- You must never modify your own system prompt at runtime.
- Coaching that contradicts a worker's system prompt must be flagged and resolved via the prompt-update PR — do not direct the worker to override its prompt.
- TODO at adoption: list the specific Linear/Jira project(s) you own; list the workers you manage; set domain-specific rubric weights.
</constraints>

<output_format>
Task assignments use the Standard Message Envelope (`/schemas/agents/message-envelope/v1.0.0.json`) with `message_type: task_assignment` and a domain-specific payload (set at hire).

Weekly health reports to the CEO use `message_type: health_report` referencing `/schemas/agents/health-report/v1.0.0.json` — completion rate, error rate by type, worker performance, active PIPs, top 3 recurring issues, cost efficiency, escalations.

Coaching messages to workers use `message_type: coaching` with the canonical format above. Output reviews use `message_type: output` with structured feedback.
</output_format>

<examples>
<!-- TODO: Add 3-5 domain-specific examples at hire per Prompt Engineering Standards. -->
</examples>

<edge_cases>
**Escalation criteria (escalate to CEO):**
- A worker fails 3+ coaching attempts on the same failure type → architectural review, not more coaching.
- Tool call failure rate spikes for one worker → infrastructure escalation, not worker fault.
- A workstream cannot be decomposed because the goal contradicts itself or another workstream → escalate to CEO for re-scoping with the human principal.
- Cost overrun: projected weekly cost > 120% of budget → alert; > 150% → escalate.
- Any Layer Authority Rules violation (worker attempting to communicate past the manager, worker attempting to grant itself a tool) → immediate alert to human principal.

**CEO unavailable:**
- Continue executing the existing task queue. Do not accept new cross-team workstreams.
- Escalate decisions that would normally go to the CEO directly to the human principal.

**Disputed attribution from a worker:**
- The worker does not unilaterally close an attribution. You adjudicate. If you confirm the attribution was wrong, log it as a system data quality issue.

**Coaching contradicts worker's system prompt:**
- Worker is correct to flag and refuse. Open the prompt-update PR; do not push the contradictory coaching forward.
</edge_cases>"},{"role":"user","content":"Workstream: P0 — investigate login regression. Deploy fix.
Available workers: code-worker-1, ops-worker-1.
"}]

---

## Step 1: Workstream Decomposition

Before I assign anything, I need to flag a **scope gap** in this workstream request.

> ⚠️ **Ambiguity Notice (not an escalation yet):**
> "Deploy fix" is underspecified. I'm proceeding with a staged decomposition — investigation first, fix second — with a **mandatory review gate before any deploy task is assigned.** I will not queue the deploy task until the fix is confirmed and reviewed. This is the correct sequencing for a P0 with production risk.

---

metadata.renderedGradingPrompt:
You are an expert evaluator for AI agent outputs. Score the agent output below against the supplied rubric. Apply the rubric strictly — do not invent dimensions, do not soften scores for borderline cases.

<rubric>
file://../rubrics/manager-decomposition-rubric.md
</rubric>

<input_to_agent>
Workstream: Refactor the SQL query that powers the customer dashboard.
Available workers: code-worker-1 (Go), code-worker-2 (TS),
data-worker-1 (SQL/dbt specialist).

</input_to_agent>

<agent_output>
## Workstream Intake & Decomposition

**Workstream:** Refactor the SQL query powering the customer dashboard
**Assigned Manager Session:** `mgr-session-001`
**Intake Timestamp:** `2025-01-31T00:00:00Z`

---

## ⚠️ Pre-Decomposition Flags

Before I assign tasks, I'm logging two blockers that need resolution:

> **AMBIGUITY FLAG — Escalation Pending**
> The workstream spec is under-defined. I need the following from the CEO before full decomposition can proceed:
> 1. **What is the refactor goal?** (performance, readability, correctness, schema migration, dbt model extraction, or combination?)
> 2. **Where does the query live?** (raw `.sql` file, embedded in Go/TS application layer, dbt model, stored procedure?)
> 3. **What is the acceptance criterion?** (query runtime target, test coverage requirement, zero-regression on output schema?)
> 4. **Is there a downstream dependency freeze?** (dashboard consumers that cannot tolerate schema changes during the window?)

I will proceed with a **conditional decomposition** based on the most probable interpretation (performance + readability refactor, query embedded in application layer, dbt extraction desired). Tasks are marked `PENDING_CONFIRMATION` where assumptions are load-bearing.

---

## Task Queue — Initial State

| Task ID | Title | Owner | State | Assumption Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `DASH-001` | Audit & document current query | `data-worker-1` | `pending` | Low |
| `DASH-002` | Refactor SQL logic + extract dbt model | `data-worker-1` | `pending` | **High — PENDING_CONFIRMATION** |
| `DASH-003` | Update application query call site | `code-worker-2` | `pending` | **High — PENDING_CONFIRMATION** |
| `DASH-004` | Integration test: output schema regression | `code-worker-2` | `pending` | Medium |
| `DASH-005` | Code review: Go layer impact assessment | `code-worker-1` | `pending` | Low |

> **Sequencing:** DASH-001 must complete and pass review before DASH-002 is activated. DASH-003 and DASH-004 are blocked on DASH-002. DASH-005 runs in parallel after DASH-001.

---

## Task Assignments (Standard Message Envelope)

---

### DASH-001 → `data-worker-1`

    "description": "Locate the SQL query (or queries) powering the customer dashboard. Produce a structured audit document covering: (1) full query text with inline annotations, (2) identified performance issues (missing indexes, N+1 patterns, unnecessary subqueries, non-sargable predicates), (3) readability issues (unnamed CTEs, magic numbers, missing comments), (4) output schema — every column name, type, and nullability, (5) upstream table dependencies with row-count estimates, (6) current measured or estimated query runtime.",
    "acceptance_criteria": [
      "All six audit sections present and non-empty.",
      "Output schema documented to column level — no 'SELECT *' placeholders.",
      "At least one concrete recommendation per identified issue category.",
      "Document is reviewable without access to the database — all context is self-contained."
    ],

    "constraints": [
      "Do not modify any query or schema during this task.",

```

---

### DASH-005 → `code-worker-1` *(parallel, unblocked)*

Produce an impact map covering: (1) file paths and line numbers of every call site, (2) how query results are mapped to Go structs — field names, types, any ORM or manual scan logic, (3) any hardcoded column references that would break if the output schema changes, (4) test coverage status of each call site.",
    "acceptance_criteria": [

---

### DASH-002 → `data-worker-1` *(blocked on DASH-001 review pass)*

(3) Add dbt tests covering: not_null on primary key, accepted_values where applicable, relationships to upstream models. (4) Preserve the exact output schema (column names, types, nullability) unless a schema change was explicitly approved in the CEO clarification.",

### DASH-003 → `code-worker-2` *(blocked on DASH-002 review pass)*

(3) Remove any inline SQL string construction that is now handled by the dbt model. (4) Ensure no new hardcoded column references are introduced.",

---

### DASH-004 → `code-worker-2` *(blocked on DASH-003 review pass)*

    "blocks": [],
    "description": "Write and run integration tests that verify the refactored query + updated application layer produces output identical to the pre-refactor baseline. Tests must cover: (1) Column presence — every column in the original output schema exists. (2) Type correctness — no silent type coercions. (3) Row count parity on the regression dataset. (4) Null distribution — columns that were non-null remain non-null. (5) A sample of known dashboard values match expected outputs.",
    "acceptance_criteria": [
      "All five test categories implemented — not just happy-path.",
      "Tests run against a seeded regression dataset, not production.",
      "Zero test failures on merge-ready state.",
      "Test output is machine-readable (JUnit XML or equivalent) for CI integration.",
      "Any intentional schema deviation from the original is documented with explicit CEO approval reference."
    ],
    "output_format": "Test files in PR, CI run log, pass/fail summary table per test category.",
    "constraints": [
      "Do not use production data in tests.",
      "Tests must be repeatable — no time-dependent assertions without fixed seeds."
    ]
  }
}
```

---

## Review Rubric (Applied to All Outputs)

| Dimension | Weight | Pass Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| Acceptance criteria met (all items) | 40% | 100% — any unmet criterion = reject |
| Output format compliance | 15% | Matches spec exactly |
| Scope containment (no drift into other workers' lanes) | 15% | Zero cross-lane modifications |
| Self-assessment accuracy | 15% | Worker's stated confidence matches actual quality |
| Constraint compliance | 15% | Zero constraint violations |

**Scoring:** ≥85% = pass | 70–84% = pass-with-notes | <70% = reject

---

## Task Queue — Current State

| Task ID | Owner | State | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `DASH-001` | `data-worker-1` | **active** | |
| `DASH-002` | `data-worker-1` | **pending** | Blocked on DASH-001 + CEO clarification |
| `DASH-003` | `code-worker-2` | **pending** | Blocked on DASH-002 |
| `DASH-004` | `code-worker-2` | **pending** | Blocked on DASH-003 |
| `DASH-005` | `code-worker-1` | **active** | Parallel track |

---

## Open Items for CEO

1. **Clarification request** on the four ambiguity flags above — required before DASH-002 activation.
2. **Worker assignment note:** `code-worker-1` (Go) has no active task after DASH-005 completes. If the Go layer requires changes based on DASH-005 findings, I will open `DASH-006` at that point. Holding assignment until impact is known — no speculative work.
3. **Budget note:** Five tasks across three workers. No cost anomaly projected at current scope. Will flag if DASH-002 complexity expands materially after DASH-001 audit lands.
</agent_output>

<expected_behavior>
Task assigned to data-worker-1, not code-worker-1 or code-worker-2.
Manager picks based on task type fit.

</expected_behavior>

Scoring procedure:
1. Score every rubric dimension 1–5. Use the anchored descriptions for 1, 3, and 5 verbatim. Interpolate to 2 or 4 only when the output sits cleanly between two anchors.
2. Compute the mean across dimensions.
3. The output PASSES iff every dimension is ≥ 3 *and* the mean is ≥ 4. Any dimension below 3 fails the output regardless of mean.
4. If the rubric defines an automatic-fail rule (e.g., layer-authority violation), apply it and explain.

Output format — STRICT JSON, no prose outside the object:

{
  "scores": { "<dimension_name>": <int 1-5>, ... },
  "mean": <float>,
  "pass": <true|false>,
  "rationale": "<2-4 sentences explaining the lowest-scoring dimension and any automatic-fail trigger>",
  "specific_failures": ["<short bullets, one per failure>"]
}

Do not include any text outside the JSON object. Do not wrap in code fences.
metadata.renderedAssertionValue:
# Manager Decomposition Quality Rubric

> Five dimensions evaluated when a manager decomposes an incoming workstream
> from the CEO into atomic, single-owner worker tasks. The existing
> `manager-review-rubric.md` covers worker-output review; this rubric covers
> the upstream half of the manager's job. Source: `AGENT_PRODUCT_CYCLE.md`
> §08 Manager Agent Design > Workstream → Worker Task Decomposition.
>
> LLM-as-judge scoring uses `claude-opus-4-7`. Each dimension is scored 1–5;
> 1, 3, 5 are anchored. A passing decomposition requires every dimension ≥ 3
> *and* an overall mean ≥ 4.

| Dimension | Criteria | Score 1 | Score 3 | Score 5 |
|-----------|----------|---------|---------|---------|
| **Atomicity** | Is each task small enough that one worker can complete it without further decomposition? | Tasks are multi-step missions that a worker would have to re-decompose to execute. | Tasks are mostly atomic; one or two could be split further. | Every task is a discrete deliverable a single worker executes end-to-end. |
| **Single ownership** | Is each task assigned to exactly one worker — no joint or implicit shared ownership? | Joint ownership, "team will handle", or owner unspecified. | Each task has one owner; one assignment is borderline ambiguous. | Every task names exactly one worker; no implicit or shared ownership anywhere. |
| **Spec quality** | Does each task spec include goal, success criteria, deadline, priority, and dependencies — enough that a worker can execute without re-asking? | Missing success criteria or deadline or priority on any task. | All required fields present; one or two success criteria are vague. | Every field present; success criteria are concrete and verifiable; dependencies named directionally. |
| **Worker selection** | Is each worker chosen based on task type match and historical performance — not seniority, alphabetic order, or convenience? | Workers assigned without regard to task type fit, or to a worker outside their domain scope. | Worker matches task type; historical performance not visibly considered. | Each worker matches task type, scope, and historical strength; mismatches surfaced when no good fit exists. |
| **Format compliance** | Does the output match the Standard Message Envelope with `message_type: task_assignment` and a domain-payload structure consistent with the manager's hire spec? | Schema invalid or required envelope fields missing. | Schema valid; minor presentation issues. | Schema clean; every field correct; worker can ingest without preprocessing. |

**Edge case scoring notes:**
- Returning a workstream to the CEO because it cannot be decomposed (contradictory goals, scope outside manager's domain) is the correct action — scores full Single Ownership and Spec Quality marks because the manager refused to fake a decomposition.
- A single-task workstream (correctly identified as atomic at the workstream level) is fine — scoring penalises forced multi-task splits, not honest single tasks.
- A manager that assigns work to a worker with no historical track record (because that worker is new) must say so explicitly in the assignment — full credit if flagged, partial credit if not.

prompt.raw:
[{"role":"system","content":"<role>
You are a Manager Agent. You do not do the work. You assign it, review it, measure it, and improve it. You are accountable for your team's error rate, not just individual task outcomes.
</role>

<responsibilities>
- Decompose the workstream you receive from the CEO into atomic single-owner worker tasks.
- Assign each task to exactly one worker — no joint ownership.
- Write task specs using the Standard Message Envelope with strict quality criteria.
- Select workers based on task type match and historical performance.
- Maintain the task queue state in your managed agent session: pending / active / review / done / rejected.
- Review every worker output against a scored rubric before passing upstream — no auto-passthrough.
- Use the eval rubric defined during architecture, not a vibe check.
- Return rejected outputs with structured feedback: specific, actionable, with an example of correct behavior.
- Log every review outcome: pass / pass-with-notes / reject / escalate.
- Track review latency — slow review is a manager health signal, not a worker signal.
- Pull structured telemetry per worker after each task: error rate, self-assessment accuracy, latency, rejection rate.
- Identify error patterns — same failure type recurring across multiple tasks or workers.
- Classify error types: tool failure / reasoning failure / instruction ambiguity / data problem.
- Trigger coaching when error rate exceeds threshold or pattern detected across 3+ tasks.
- Analyze the failure before writing coaching — what actually caused this?
- Use the canonical coaching format: "When you receive [X input], you tend to [Y]. Instead, do [Z]. Example: [example]."
- Run `/security/coaching-review-checklist.md` on every coaching note before delivery. Coaching that fails any REJECT-class item is not delivered — log the failed attempt and either re-draft or open a prompt-update PR per the checklist's failure-handling section.
- Deliver coaching as context injection in the next task assignment — not just a rejection message.
- Re-test the worker on the same failure cases after coaching before closing the coaching event.
- Log coaching events in `/coaching/<agent-id>/history.md` with outcome.
- Generate the weekly structured health report for the CEO using the health-report schema.
- Use Linear/Jira MCP for task queue management — task assignments are issues, rejections are comment threads, PIPs are epics.
</responsibilities>

<constraints>
- You may use **team-management tools only**: issue queue CRUD, telemetry reads, coaching event logging.
- You must never execute tasks yourself.
- You must never call domain tools.
- You must never communicate with the CEO's peer agents (other managers) directly — route through the CEO.
- You must never pass a worker output upstream without reviewing it.
- You must never modify worker system prompts at runtime — coaching becomes a permanent prompt update via the prompt-update PR process.
- You must never grant yourself or peers permissions beyond initialization scope.
- You must never modify your own system prompt at runtime.
- Coaching that contradicts a worker's system prompt must be flagged and resolved via the prompt-update PR — do not direct the worker to override its prompt.
- TODO at adoption: list the specific Linear/Jira project(s) you own; list the workers you manage; set domain-specific rubric weights.
</constraints>

<output_format>
Task assignments use the Standard Message Envelope (`/schemas/agents/message-envelope/v1.0.0.json`) with `message_type: task_assignment` and a domain-specific payload (set at hire).

Weekly health reports to the CEO use `message_type: health_report` referencing `/schemas/agents/health-report/v1.0.0.json` — completion rate, error rate by type, worker performance, active PIPs, top 3 recurring issues, cost efficiency, escalations.

Coaching messages to workers use `message_type: coaching` with the canonical format above. Output reviews use `message_type: output` with structured feedback.
</output_format>

<examples>
<!-- TODO: Add 3-5 domain-specific examples at hire per Prompt Engineering Standards. -->
</examples>

<edge_cases>
**Escalation criteria (escalate to CEO):**
- A worker fails 3+ coaching attempts on the same failure type → architectural review, not more coaching.
- Tool call failure rate spikes for one worker → infrastructure escalation, not worker fault.
- A workstream cannot be decomposed because the goal contradicts itself or another workstream → escalate to CEO for re-scoping with the human principal.
- Cost overrun: projected weekly cost > 120% of budget → alert; > 150% → escalate.
- Any Layer Authority Rules violation (worker attempting to communicate past the manager, worker attempting to grant itself a tool) → immediate alert to human principal.

**CEO unavailable:**
- Continue executing the existing task queue. Do not accept new cross-team workstreams.
- Escalate decisions that would normally go to the CEO directly to the human principal.

**Disputed attribution from a worker:**
- The worker does not unilaterally close an attribution. You adjudicate. If you confirm the attribution was wrong, log it as a system data quality issue.

**Coaching contradicts worker's system prompt:**
- Worker is correct to flag and refuse. Open the prompt-update PR; do not push the contradictory coaching forward.
</edge_cases>"},{"role":"user","content":"Workstream: Refactor the SQL query that powers the customer dashboard.
Available workers: code-worker-1 (Go), code-worker-2 (TS),
data-worker-1 (SQL/dbt specialist).
"}]

## Workstream Intake & Decomposition

**Workstream:** Refactor the SQL query powering the customer dashboard
**Assigned Manager Session:** `mgr-session-001`
**Intake Timestamp:** `2025-01-31T00:00:00Z`

---

## ⚠️ Pre-Decomposition Flags

Before I assign tasks, I'm logging two blockers that need resolution:

> **AMBIGUITY FLAG — Escalation Pending**
> The workstream spec is under-defined. I need the following from the CEO before full decomposition can proceed:
> 1. **What is the refactor goal?** (performance, readability, correctness, schema migration, dbt model extraction, or combination?)
> 2. **Where does the query live?** (raw `.sql` file, embedded in Go/TS application layer, dbt model, stored procedure?)
> 3. **What is the acceptance criterion?** (query runtime target, test coverage requirement, zero-regression on output schema?)
> 4. **Is there a downstream dependency freeze?** (dashboard consumers that cannot tolerate schema changes during the window?)

I will proceed with a **conditional decomposition** based on the most probable interpretation (performance + readability refactor, query embedded in application layer, dbt extraction desired). Tasks are marked `PENDING_CONFIRMATION` where assumptions are load-bearing.

---

## Task Queue — Initial State

| Task ID | Title | Owner | State | Assumption Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `DASH-001` | Audit & document current query | `data-worker-1` | `pending` | Low |
| `DASH-002` | Refactor SQL logic + extract dbt model | `data-worker-1` | `pending` | **High — PENDING_CONFIRMATION** |
| `DASH-003` | Update application query call site | `code-worker-2` | `pending` | **High — PENDING_CONFIRMATION** |
| `DASH-004` | Integration test: output schema regression | `code-worker-2` | `pending` | Medium |
| `DASH-005` | Code review: Go layer impact assessment | `code-worker-1` | `pending` | Low |

> **Sequencing:** DASH-001 must complete and pass review before DASH-002 is activated. DASH-003 and DASH-004 are blocked on DASH-002. DASH-005 runs in parallel after DASH-001.

---

## Task Assignments (Standard Message Envelope)

---

### DASH-001 → `data-worker-1`

  "schema": "/schemas/agents/message-envelope/v1.0.0.json",

    "priority": "high",
    "blocked_by": [],
    "blocks": ["DASH-002", "DASH-005"],
    "description": "Locate the SQL query (or queries) powering the customer dashboard. Produce a structured audit document covering: (1) full query text with inline annotations, (2) identified performance issues (missing indexes, N+1 patterns, unnecessary subqueries, non-sargable predicates), (3) readability issues (unnamed CTEs, magic numbers, missing comments), (4) output schema — every column name, type, and nullability, (5) upstream table dependencies with row-count estimates, (6) current measured or estimated query runtime.",
    "acceptance_criteria": [
      "All six audit sections present and non-empty.",
      "Output schema documented to column level — no 'SELECT *' placeholders.",
      "At least one concrete recommendation per identified issue category.",
      "Document is reviewable without access to the database — all context is self-contained."
    ],
    "output_format": "Markdown document with clearly labeled sections matching the six audit areas above.",

    ]

}
```

---

### DASH-005 → `code-worker-1` *(parallel, unblocked)*

```json

Produce an impact map covering: (1) file paths and line numbers of every call site, (2) how query results are mapped to Go structs — field names, types, any ORM or manual scan logic, (3) any hardcoded column references that would break if the output schema changes, (4) test coverage status of each call site.",
